content: I'll start by reading my role and task files.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)
18	
19	The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:
20	
21	- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
22	- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
23	- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
24	- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").
25	
26	The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.
27	
28	## Protected declarations
29	
30	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
31	
32	## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)
33	
34	`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:
35	
36	- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
37	- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.
38	
39	## Avoid early termination
40	
41	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
42	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
43	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
44	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
45	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
46	
47	## Task completion criteria
48	
49	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
50	
51	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
52	2. Zero axioms introduced.
53	3. The file compiles cleanly.
54	
55	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
56	
57	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
58	
59	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
60	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
61	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
62	
63	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
64	
65	## When infrastructure is missing
66	
67	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
68	
69	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
70	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
71	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
72	
73	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
74	
75	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
76	
77	## Proof style
78	
79	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
80	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
81	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
82	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
83	- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
84	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
85

94	## LSP MCP tools
95	
96	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
97	
98	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
99	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
100	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
101	
102	## Search protocol
103	
104	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
105	
106	1. `lean_local_search` first.
107	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
108	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
109	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
110	
111	## Tooling traps
112	
113	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
114	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.RegroupHelper
8	
9	/-!
10	# Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf (`i = 0`)
11	
12	This file establishes the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of flat base change: the
13	formation of the pushforward `f_* F` of a quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat
14	base change on the target.
15	
16	Throughout we work with a (commutative, eventually cartesian) square of schemes
17	```
18	  X' --g'--> X
19	  |f'        |f
20	  v          v
21	  S' --g---> S
22	```
23	recorded by morphisms `f : X ⟶ S`, `g : S' ⟶ S`, `f' : X' ⟶ S'`, `g' : X' ⟶ X`
24	with `g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`, and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of modules on `X`.
25	
26	The three main declarations are:
27	
28	* `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap` — the canonical base-change map
29	  `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)`, built as the adjoint mate of the unit of the
30	  `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction.
31	* `AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` — for `f` affine and the
32	  square cartesian, the base-change map is an isomorphism (affine case: tensor
33	  associativity).
34	* `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso` — for `g` flat and `f`
35	  quasi-compact quasi-separated, the base-change map is an isomorphism.
36	
37	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`.
38	
39	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
40	Tag 02KH.
41	-/
42	
43	universe u
44	
45	open CategoryTheory Limits
46	
47	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
48	
49	open Scheme.Modules
50	
51	open scoped TensorProduct
52	
53	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
54	  (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
55	
56	/-- The canonical base-change map for the pushforward of a sheaf of modules.
57	
58	Given a commutative square
59	```
60	  X' --g'--> X
61	  |f'        |f
62	  v          v
63	  S' --g---> S
64	```
65	(with `comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`) and a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`, this is the
66	canonical morphism `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)` of sheaves of modules on `S'`.
67	
68	It is the image, under the `(g^*, g_*)`-adjunction transpose, of the composite
69	```
70	  f_* F --f_*(unit)--> f_* (g')_* (g')^* F
71	        = (g' ≫ f)_* (g')^* F = (f' ≫ g)_* (g')^* F = g_* f'_* (g')^* F,
72	```
73	where `unit` is the unit of the `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction and the middle
74	equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward together with the
75	commutativity of the square.
76	
77	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
78	base-change diagram. -/
79	noncomputable def pushforwardBaseChangeMap (comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g) (F : X.Modules) :
80	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj ((pushforward f).obj F) ⟶
81	      (pushforward f').obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F) :=
82	  ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).homEquiv _ _).symm
83	    ((pushforward f).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g').unit.app F) ≫
84	      (pushforwardComp g' f).hom.app _ ≫
85	      (pushforwardCongr comm).hom.app _ ≫
86	      (pushforwardComp f' g).inv.app _)
87	
88	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — locality of isomorphisms for `Scheme.Modules`
89	
90	Mathlib provides the per-open criterion `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`
91	(`IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U, IsIso (φ.app U)`) and the stalkwise criterion
92	`TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso` for `TopCat.Sheaf`-valued morphisms,
93	but it does not package the stalk-local criterion at the level of `Scheme.Modules`
94	morphisms. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they are the locality tools needed for
95	the affine reduction of `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (where one checks the
96	base-change map after restricting to / taking stalks over affine opens). -/
97	
98	/-- **Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** A morphism `φ : M ⟶ N`
99	of sheaves of modules over a scheme `X` is an isomorphism iff its underlying morphism of
100	abelian presheaves is a stalkwise isomorphism. Project-local: Mathlib only ships the
101	per-open criterion and the `TopCat.Sheaf`-level stalk criterion separately. -/
102	theorem Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
103	    (φ : M ⟶ N) :
104	    IsIso φ ↔ ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map
105	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ)) := by
106	  constructor
107	  · intro h x
108	    haveI : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) := Functor.map_isIso _ _
109	    exact Functor.map_isIso _ _
110	  · intro h
111	    -- Package the underlying abelian presheaves as `TopCat.Sheaf`es.
112	    let MS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩
113	    let NS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨N.presheaf, N.isSheaf⟩
114	    let fS : MS ⟶ NS := ⟨(Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ⟩
115	    haveI : ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map fS.hom) := h
116	    haveI hSiso : IsIso fS := TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso fS
117	    have h1 : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) :=
118	      (TopCat.Sheaf.forget Ab.{u} X).map_isIso fS
119	    exact (CategoryTheory.isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso φ (Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X)).mp h1
120	
121	/-- **Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** If `B` is a basis of
122	opens of `X` and `φ : M ⟶ N` restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every basic
123	open `B i`, then `φ` is an isomorphism. This reduces iso-checking from *all* opens (the
124	content of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`) to a chosen basis. Project-local:

291	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj ((moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).obj N) := by
292	  set SecN : ModuleCat ↑((Spec R').ringCatSheaf.obj.obj (Opposite.op ⊤)) :=
293	    (((SheafOfModules.forgetToSheafModuleCat (Spec R').ringCatSheaf (Opposite.op ⊤)
294	        (Limits.initialOpOfTerminal Limits.isTerminalTop)).obj N).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤)) with hSecN
295	  set gsRhom := (StructureSheaf.globalSectionsIso ↑R).hom.hom with hgsR

305	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App φ.hom gsR'hom (gsR'hom.comp φ.hom) rfl SecN)

371	        (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom] :

375	  rw [ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]

377	        (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app N.fromTildeΓ (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a))) =
378	      ⇑(ConcreteCategory.hom
379	        ((modulesSpecToSheaf.map N.fromTildeΓ).1.app (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)))) :=
380	    rfl
381	  rw [hfun]
382	  -- Abbreviations: `j` is the tilde localization map, `ρ` the structure-sheaf restriction, both
383	  -- localizations of `Γ(N, ⊤)` at `powers a`; `L` is the section map of the counit.
384	  set j := (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj N).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤))
385	    (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)).hom with hj
386	  set ρ := ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj N).val.map
387	    (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom with hρ

389	  clear_value ρ

391	    (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a))).hom with hL
392	  -- The triangle identity gives `L ∘ₗ j = ρ`.
393	  have htri : L ∘ₗ j = ρ := by
394	    have := Scheme.Modules.toOpen_fromTildeΓ_app N (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen a)
395	    apply_fun ModuleCat.Hom.hom at this
396	    simpa [hL, hj, hρ, ModuleCat.hom_comp] using this
397	  -- `L` equals the canonical iso between the two localizations of `Γ(N, ⊤)`, hence bijective.
398	  set ej := IsLocalizedModule.iso (Submonoid.powers a) j with hej
399	  set eρ := IsLocalizedModule.iso (Submonoid.powers a) ρ with heρ

401	  have hcomp : e.toLinearMap ∘ₗ j = ρ := by
402	    ext x
403	    simp only [e, hej, heρ, LinearMap.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply, LinearEquiv.coe_coe,

407	    IsLocalizedModule.ext (Submonoid.powers a) j (IsLocalizedModule.map_units ρ)

413	/-- **Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional on the basic-open localization fact.**
414	This is the full assembly of `pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` (route iii), modulo the single
415	remaining ingredient: that on each basic open `D(a)` the structure-sheaf restriction of the
416	pushforward `(Spec φ)_* M^~` exhibits its sections over `D(a)` as the localization at
417	`Submonoid.powers a` of its global sections. Given that hypothesis (`hloc`), the counit
418	`fromTildeΓ ((Spec φ)_* M^~)` is an isomorphism (basis-locality criterion
419	`Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis` over the basic opens, with each section iso supplied by
420	`fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule`), so `(Spec φ)_* M^~` lies in the essential image of
421	`tilde` and the global-sections comparison `gammaPushforwardTildeIso` upgrades to the desired
422	object isomorphism.

424	The hypothesis `hloc` is the *only* outstanding obligation for the unconditional
425	`pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`: it is the affine "`(Spec φ)⁻¹ D(a) = D(φ a)`, and localizing
426	`restrict φ M` at `a` agrees with localizing `M` at `φ a`" fact (Stacks, widetilde-pullback),
427	whose ring-change core is `IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars` below. Discharging it
428	requires identifying the `modulesSpecToSheaf` global-ring `R`-action on the sections of the
429	pushforward over `D(a)` with restriction of scalars along `φ` of the `R'`-localization
430	`M[1/φ a]` — the section-level structure-sheaf naturality at `D(a)`. -/
431	noncomputable def pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule {R R' : CommRingCat.{u}}
432	    (φ : R ⟶ R') (M : ModuleCat.{u} R')

448	/-- **Restriction of scalars of a localized module is a localized module.** If `f : M →ₗ[A] N`
449	exhibits `N` as the localization of `M` at the image submonoid `Algebra.algebraMapSubmonoid A S`
450	(for `S : Submonoid R` and `A` an `R`-algebra), then the `R`-linear map underlying `f`
451	exhibits `N` as the localization of `M` at `S` itself. This is the exact converse of
452	`IsLocalizedModule.of_restrictScalars` and is the ring-change ingredient powering the affine
453	pushforward identification: localizing `restrictScalars φ M` at `a ∈ R` agrees with localizing
454	`M` at `φ a ∈ R'`. Project-local: Mathlib ships only the forward direction. -/
455	lemma IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars
456	    {R A : Type*} [CommSemiring R] [CommSemiring A] [Algebra R A]
457	    {M N : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M] [AddCommMonoid N]
458	    [Module R M] [Module R N] [Module A M] [Module A N]
459	    [IsScalarTower R A M] [IsScalarTower R A N]
460	    (S : Submonoid R) (f : M →ₗ[A] N)
461	    [IsLocalizedModule (Algebra.algebraMapSubmonoid A S) f] :
462	    IsLocalizedModule S (f.restrictScalars R) where
463	  map_units x := by
464	    have h := IsLocalizedModule.map_units f
465	      (⟨algebraMap R A x, Submonoid.mem_map.mpr ⟨x, x.2, rfl⟩⟩ : Algebra.algebraMapSubmonoid A S)
466	    simp only [← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_apply, Module.End.isUnit_iff] at h ⊢
467	    exact h
468	  surj y := by
469	    obtain ⟨⟨x, ⟨_, t, ht, rfl⟩⟩, e⟩ := IsLocalizedModule.surj (Algebra.algebraMapSubmonoid A S) f y
470	    exact ⟨⟨x, ⟨t, ht⟩⟩, by simpa [Submonoid.smul_def, IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul] using e⟩
471	  exists_of_eq {x₁ x₂} e := by
472	    obtain ⟨⟨_, c, hc, rfl⟩, h⟩ :=
473	      IsLocalizedModule.exists_of_eq (S := Algebra.algebraMapSubmonoid A S) (f := f) e
474	    exact ⟨⟨c, hc⟩, by simpa [Submonoid.smul_def, IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul] using h⟩
475	
476	/-- **The structure-sheaf restriction of a tilde-module from `⊤` to `D(b)` is a localization.**
477	Read in `ModuleCat R'` via `modulesSpecToSheaf`, the restriction map
478	`Γ(M^~, ⊤) → Γ(M^~, D(b))` exhibits its target as the localization of its source at
479	`Submonoid.powers b`. This packages the Mathlib instance
480	`tilde.toOpen … (basicOpen b)` (which makes `M → Γ(M^~, D(b))` a localization) together with the
481	triangle identity `tilde.toOpen_res` and the fact that `M → Γ(M^~, ⊤)` is bijective (localization
482	at the trivial submonoid `powers 1`). Project-local. -/
483	lemma tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule {R' : CommRingCat.{u}} (M : ModuleCat.{u} R') (b : R') :
484	    IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers b)
485	      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.map
486	        (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen b ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom := by
487	  -- `toOpen M ⊤` is a localization at `powers (1 : R')` (since `basicOpen 1 = ⊤`), hence bijective.
488	  haveI inst1 : IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (1 : R')) (tilde.toOpen M ⊤).hom := by
489	    have h := (inferInstance : IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (1 : R'))
490	      (tilde.toOpen M (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen (1 : R'))).hom)
491	    rw [PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen_one] at h
492	    exact h
493	  have hbij : Function.Bijective (tilde.toOpen M ⊤).hom := by
494	    refine ⟨fun x y hxy => ?_, fun y => ?_⟩
495	    · obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := IsLocalizedModule.exists_of_eq
496	        (S := Submonoid.powers (1 : R')) (f := (tilde.toOpen M ⊤).hom) hxy
497	      obtain ⟨n, hn⟩ := c.2
498	      have hc1 : (↑c : R') = 1 := by simpa using hn.symm
499	      rw [Submonoid.smul_def, Submonoid.smul_def, hc1, one_smul, one_smul] at hc
500	      exact hc
501	    · obtain ⟨⟨x, c⟩, hc⟩ := IsLocalizedModule.surj
502	        (Submonoid.powers (1 : R')) (tilde.toOpen M ⊤).hom y
503	      obtain ⟨n, hn⟩ := c.2
504	      have hc1 : (↑c : R') = 1 := by simpa using hn.symm
505	      refine ⟨x, ?_⟩
506	      rw [Submonoid.smul_def, hc1, one_smul] at hc
507	      exact hc.symm
508	  -- Triangle identity: `toOpen ⊤ ≫ (restriction) = toOpen (D b)`.
509	  have htri := tilde.toOpen_res M (⊤ : (Spec R').Opens) (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen b)
510	    (homOfLE (le_top))
511	  -- The inverse equivalence `Γ(M^~, ⊤) ≃ M`.
512	  set le : M ≃ₗ[R'] ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤)) :=
513	    LinearEquiv.ofBijective (tilde.toOpen M ⊤).hom hbij with hle
514	  -- The triangle at the level of linear maps: `restriction ∘ₗ le = toOpen (D b)`.
515	  have htri2 : ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.map
516	        (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen b ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom ∘ₗ le.toLinearMap
517	      = (tilde.toOpen M (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen b)).hom := by
518	    have h := congrArg ModuleCat.Hom.hom htri
519	    rw [ModuleCat.hom_comp] at h
520	    exact h
521	  -- Hence `restriction = toOpen (D b) ∘ₗ le⁻¹`, a localization at `powers b` precomposed with
522	  -- a linear equivalence.
523	  have key : ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.map
524	        (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen b ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op).hom
525	      = (tilde.toOpen M (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen b)).hom ∘ₗ le.symm.toLinearMap :=
526	    (LinearEquiv.eq_comp_toLinearMap_symm _ _).mpr htri2
527	  rw [key]
528	  exact IsLocalizedModule.of_linearEquiv_right (Submonoid.powers b)
529	    (tilde.toOpen M (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen b)).hom le.symm

531	/-- **Affine pushforward of a tilde-module (unconditional).** For a ring map `φ : R ⟶ R'`
532	and an `R'`-module `M`, pushing the quasi-coherent sheaf `M^~` forward along `Spec φ` is,
533	up to canonical isomorphism, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of `M` along `φ`. This
534	is the unconditional form of `pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule`: the latter's
535	hypothesis `hloc` (each structure-sheaf restriction `Γ(N,⊤) → Γ(N,D(a))` is a localization at
536	`powers a`) is discharged here by the `D(a)`-level transport. See blueprint
537	`lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`. -/
538	noncomputable def pushforward_spec_tilde_iso {R R' : CommRingCat.{u}}
539	    (φ : R ⟶ R') (M : ModuleCat.{u} R') :
540	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde M) ≅
541	      tilde ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj M) := by
542	  apply pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule φ M
543	  intro a
544	  -- STRATEGY (`of_linearEquiv`): the restriction map `ρ : Γ(N,⊤) → Γ(N,D(a))` of
545	  -- `N := (Spec φ)_* (M^~)` is, under the isomorphisms
546	  -- `gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) ⊤` and `gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) (D a)`,
547	  -- identified with the restriction of scalars along `φ` of the `R'`-side tilde restriction
548	  -- `σ : Γ(M^~, ⊤) → Γ(M^~, D(φ a))`. That `σ` is a localization at `powers (φ a)`
549	  -- (`tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule`), so its restriction of scalars along `φ` is a
550	  -- localization at `powers a` (`IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars`); transporting along
551	  -- the two isomorphisms then yields `hloc(a)`.
552	  -- The two bricks for this discharge are now in place and axiom-clean:
553	  --   • `gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) U : Γ(N, U) ≅ restrictScalars φ (Γ(M^~, (Spec φ)⁻¹ U))`
554	  --     — the open-indexed `e_{D(a)}` isomorphism (blueprint movement (1)); and
555	  --   • `tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule M (φ a)` — the `R'`-side restriction
556	  --     `Γ(M^~, ⊤) → Γ(M^~, D(φ a))` is a localization at `powers (φ a)`.
557	  -- The `of_linearEquiv` finish is: with `e₁ = gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) ⊤` and
558	  -- `e₂ = gammaPushforwardIsoAt φ (tilde M) (D a)`, the naturality square
559	  -- `e₂.hom ∘ ρ = (restrictScalars φ σ) ∘ e₁.hom` (where `σ` is the `R'`-side restriction and
560	  -- `ρ` the pushforward-side one) gives `ρ = e₂.symm ∘ₗ (restrictScalars φ σ) ∘ₗ e₁`; then
561	  -- `IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars` upgrades `σ` (localization at `powers (φ a)`,
562	  -- since `algebraMapSubmonoid R' (powers a) = powers (φ a)` along `φ.hom.toAlgebra`) to a
563	  -- localization at `powers a` of `restrictScalars φ σ`, and two applications of
564	  -- `IsLocalizedModule.of_linearEquiv` / `of_linearEquiv_right` transport this to `ρ`.
565	  --
566	  -- iter-240 PIVOT (`algebraize`): preimage opens are *definitionally* the basic opens
567	  -- (`AlgebraicGeometry.SpecMap_preimage_basicOpen` is `rfl`), so the `R'`-side restriction
568	  -- `σ` is literally `tilde M`'s restriction `⊤ → D(φ a)`, and `algebraize [φ.hom]` supplies the
569	  -- honest `Algebra ↑R ↑R'`/`IsScalarTower` instances that `powers_restrictScalars` needs.
570	  algebraize [φ.hom]
571	  -- `σ` : the `R'`-side restriction map `Γ(M^~, ⊤) → Γ(M^~, D(φ a))` of `tilde M`.

573	      (homOfLE (show PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen (φ.hom a) ≤ ⊤ from le_top)).op with hσmor
574	  -- Re-expose the `compHom`-via-`φ` `R`-module/scalar-tower instances (the ones the

577	  letI mTop : Module R ↑((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤)) :=
578	    inferInstanceAs (Module R ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj
579	      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤))))
580	  letI mDa : Module R ↑((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.obj
581	      (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen (φ.hom a)))) :=
582	    inferInstanceAs (Module R ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj
583	      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.obj (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen (φ.hom a))))))

585	    ⟨fun x y z => by rw [Algebra.smul_def, mul_smul]; rfl⟩
586	  haveI tDa : IsScalarTower R R' ↑((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).val.obj
587	      (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen (φ.hom a)))) :=
588	    ⟨fun x y z => by rw [Algebra.smul_def, mul_smul]; rfl⟩

623	        (StructureSheaf.globalSectionsIso (↑R : CommRingCat)).hom.hom).map
624	      ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars

626	  -- The open-naturality square `ρ ≫ e₂.hom = e₁.hom ≫ Gmor` (the residual `hsq`).

671	    intro N N' g

675	/-- **Affine pullback of a tilde-module.** For a ring map `φ : R ⟶ R'` and an `R`-module `M`,
676	pulling the quasi-coherent sheaf `M^~` back along `Spec φ` is, up to canonical isomorphism, the
677	tilde of the extension of scalars `R' ⊗_R M`. This is the pullback companion of
678	`pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` and is part (1) of Stacks Tag 01I9 (`lemma-widetilde-pullback`).
679	
680	The construction is the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints route. Pullback along `Spec φ` is left adjoint
681	to pushforward, and `tilde` is left adjoint to global sections; their composite
682	`tilde_R ⋙ pullback` is left adjoint to `pushforward ⋙ Γ_R`. Symmetrically `extendScalars φ` is
683	left adjoint to `restrictScalars φ`, so `extendScalars ⋙ tilde_{R'}` is left adjoint to
684	`Γ_{R'} ⋙ restrictScalars φ`. The two right adjoints are identified by the natural isomorphism

688	blueprint `lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso`. -/
689	noncomputable def pullback_spec_tilde_iso {R R' : CommRingCat.{u}}
690	    (φ : R ⟶ R') (M : ModuleCat.{u} R) :
691	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde M) ≅
692	      tilde ((ModuleCat.extendScalars φ.hom).obj M) :=
693	  let adjL := (tilde.adjunction (R := R)).comp
694	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (Spec.map φ))
695	  let adjR := (ModuleCat.extendRestrictScalarsAdj φ.hom).comp (tilde.adjunction (R := R'))
696	  (((conjugateIsoEquiv adjL adjR).symm (gammaPushforwardNatIso φ)).symm).app M

700	/-- **The pullback cone legs are the `Spec`-maps of the tensor inclusions.** For ring maps
701	`ψ : R ⟶ R'` and `φ : R ⟶ A` (regarding `A`, `R'` as `R`-algebras via `φ`, `ψ`), over the generic
702	pullback square `Limits.pullback (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)` Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso` identifies
703	the total space with `Spec (A ⊗[R] R')`, and under it the two cone legs are the `Spec`-maps of the
704	canonical tensor inclusions `Algebra.TensorProduct.includeLeftRingHom : A → A ⊗[R] R'` and
705	`Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight : R' → A ⊗[R] R'`. The only content beyond Mathlib's
706	`pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst`/`_inv_snd` is the structure bridging `Spec.map φ ↔ Spec.map (algebraMap

708	`lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor`. -/
709	theorem pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor {R R' A : CommRingCat.{u}} (ψ : R ⟶ R') (φ : R ⟶ A) :
710	    letI : Algebra R A := φ.hom.toAlgebra
711	    letI : Algebra R R' := ψ.hom.toAlgebra
712	    ((pullbackSpecIso (R := ↑R) (S := ↑A) (T := ↑R')).inv ≫
713	        Limits.pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)
714	        = Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom Algebra.TensorProduct.includeLeftRingHom)) ∧
715	      ((pullbackSpecIso (R := ↑R) (S := ↑A) (T := ↑R')).inv ≫
716	        Limits.pullback.snd (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)
717	        = Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom
718	          (Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight (R := ↑R) (A := ↑A) (B := ↑R')).toRingHom)) := by
719	  letI : Algebra R A := φ.hom.toAlgebra

722	  · exact pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst ↑R ↑A ↑R'
723	  · exact pullbackSpecIso_inv_snd ↑R ↑A ↑R'

727	/-- **Domain read of the section-level base-change map.** In the affine–affine model
728	(`g = Spec ψ`, `f = Spec φ`, `F = tilde M`), the global sections of the domain
729	`g^*(f_*(tilde M))` of the base-change map are canonically `R' ⊗_R M` as an `R'`-module — i.e.
730	the extension of scalars along `ψ` of the restriction of scalars along `φ` of `M`. The iso
731	`Θ_src` is the composite of the pushforward dictionary `pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` (reading
732	`f_*(tilde M)` as `restrictScalars φ M`) followed by the pullback dictionary
733	`pullback_spec_tilde_iso` (reading `g^*` of a tilde as extension of scalars along `ψ`), then the
734	tilde–Γ unit. The cospan maps `f = Spec φ`, `g = Spec ψ` are genuine `Spec`-maps, so the two
735	dictionaries apply directly with no pullback-leg identification needed. See blueprint
736	`lem:base_change_mate_domain_read`. -/
737	noncomputable def base_change_mate_domain_read {R R' A : CommRingCat.{u}}
738	    (ψ : R ⟶ R') (φ : R ⟶ A) (M : ModuleCat.{u} A) :
739	    (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).obj
740	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Spec.map ψ)).obj

742	      (ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ.hom).obj ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj M) :=

744	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Spec.map ψ)).mapIso (pushforward_spec_tilde_iso φ M) ≪≫

749	/-- **Pullback of modules along an isomorphism of schemes is an equivalence.** Project-local
750	helper: the inverse is `pullback (inv f)`, with unit/counit assembled from `pullbackComp` and
751	`pullbackId`. Used to recognise that pulling back along `pullbackSpecIso.hom` (an iso) and pushing
752	forward again returns the original module. -/
753	noncomputable def pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIso f] :
754	    Y.Modules ≌ X.Modules :=
755	  CategoryTheory.Equivalence.mk (Scheme.Modules.pullback f) (Scheme.Modules.pullback (inv f))
756	    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y).symm ≪≫
757	      Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (IsIso.inv_hom_id f).symm ≪≫
758	        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (inv f) f).symm)
759	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f (inv f) ≪≫
760	      Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (IsIso.hom_inv_id f) ≪≫ Scheme.Modules.pullbackId X)

762	instance pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIso f] :
763	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback f).IsEquivalence :=
764	  (pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso f).isEquivalence_functor

779	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))).obj
780	            (tilde M))) ≅
781	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars
782	          (Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight (R := (R : Type u)) (A := (A : Type u))
783	            (B := (R' : Type u))).toRingHom).obj
784	        ((ModuleCat.extendScalars
785	          (Algebra.TensorProduct.includeLeftRingHom (R := (R : Type u)) (A := (A : Type u))

791	    (R := (R : Type u)) (A := (A : Type u)) (B := (R' : Type u))) with hinclA

793	    (R := (R : Type u)) (A := (A : Type u)) (B := (R' : Type u))).toRingHom with hinclR'

1423	  sheaves of modules transports, under the tilde dictionaries which identify

1425	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) and \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\) of a tilde

1427	  extension/restriction adjunction \((\iota_A^{\,*} \dashv \iota_{A\,*})\) on
1428	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\). The dictionaries being the comparison isomorphisms of these
1429	  adjunctions, naturality identifies the two units; evaluating at \(M\) gives the stated
1430	  value \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).

1725	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
1726	  % NOTE (iter-002): the Lean decl formalizes the `IsIso (Γ(α))` *corollary* of
1727	  % this lemma, not the literal equality `Θ_tgt ∘ Γ(α) ∘ Θ_src⁻¹ = cancelBaseChange⁻¹`.
1728	  % The IsIso form is faithful and non-vacuous (it is exactly what the affine close
1729	  % consumes via lem:base_change_map_affine_local); stating the equality requires first
1730	  % identifying pullback.fst/snd over the generic square with the Spec-of-tensor
1731	  % inclusions (pullbackSpecIso), which is the same plumbing that blocks the proof.
1732	  % NOTE: the DIRECT proof body of this decl assembles the IsIso conclusion by
1733	  % conjugation; the residual obligation lives in the leaf
1734	  % lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace, which is now a one-line corollary of the
1735	  % section identity lem:base_change_mate_section_identity.
1736	  % NOTE: proof decomposed into a \uses-linked chain. The genuine crux ---
1737	  % the pullbackSpecIso identification of the cone legs --- is isolated as
1738	  % lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor; the section-level computation is the single
1739	  % identity lem:base_change_mate_section_identity (the base-change map on sections is the
1740	  % R'-base change of the algebraic unit), read through the domain read
1741	  % (lem:base_change_mate_domain_read) and codomain read
1742	  % (lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read) and packaged as IsIso by
1743	  % lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace. The two proved tilde dictionaries feed those
1744	  % reads; the regrouping isomorphism (lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, native R'-linear
1745	  % via Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange) closes the iso, matching Mathlib's
1746	  % cancelBaseChange (lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib).
1747	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1748	  lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace,
1749	  lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1750	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1751	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1752	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1753	  % L927--938).
1754	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
1755	  % to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
1756	  % Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
1757	  % $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
1758	  % to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
1759	  % Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1760	  % equality
1761	  % $$
1762	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1763	  % $$
1764	  % as $R'$-modules."
1765	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1766	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1767	  In the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
1768	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
1769	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) — with \(g = \operatorname{Spec}(\psi)\) for
1770	  \(\psi : R \to R'\), \(f = \operatorname{Spec}(\varphi)\) for \(\varphi : R \to A\),
1771	  and \(M\) an \(A\)-module — write \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) for the global-sections
1772	  incarnation of the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
1773	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}). Recall \(\alpha\) runs
1774	  \(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}) \to f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\). Transport \(\Gamma(\alpha)\)
1775	  through the two proved affine dictionaries that identify its domain and codomain: the
1776	  pushforward dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, followed by the
1777	  pullback dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, identifies the domain
1778	  \(\Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M})\bigr)\) with \(R' \otimes_R M\); the pullback
1779	  dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, followed by the pushforward
1780	  dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, identifies the codomain
1781	  \(\Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\bigr)\) with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\),
1782	  both read as \(R'\)-modules. Under these identifications \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is
1783	  precisely the canonical cancellation isomorphism
1784	  \[
1785	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1786	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
1787	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
1788	  \]
1789	  (Mathlib's \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}; here
1790	  \(a \cdot m\) is the \(A\)-action on \(M\)). Equivalently, in the direction in which
1791	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is presented — from the source \(R' \otimes_R M\) to the target
1792	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) — it is \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\),
1793	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\); the two readings agree because
1794	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism. Since
1795	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with \emph{no} flatness
1796	  hypothesis, this identification closes the affine case: \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an
1797	  isomorphism, hence (full faithfulness / counit isomorphism of \(\widetilde{(-)}\) on
1798	  quasi-coherent modules) so is \(\alpha\) on the affine chart.
1799	
1800	  \medskip
1801	  \emph{Intended formalized signature.} In the Lean affine--affine model — the square
1802	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\) maps induced by \(\psi : R \to R'\) and
1803	  \(\varphi : R \to A\), with \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\),
1804	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), and \(h\) the cartesian-square witness — the
1805	  statement is the equality of \(R'\)-linear maps
1806	  \[
1807	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma\bigl(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\
1808	      f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\ \widetilde{M}\bigr) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
1809	    \;=\;
1810	    \bigl(\texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}\bigr)^{-1},
1811	  \]
1812	  where \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}} : \Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M})\bigr)
1813	  \xrightarrow{\sim} R' \otimes_R M\) is assembled from
1814	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and
1815	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, and
1816	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} : \Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\bigr)
1817	  \xrightarrow{\sim} (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) is assembled from the same two
1818	  dictionaries; equivalently, after pre/post-composing with the dictionary isos,
1819	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) \emph{is} the cancellation isomorphism. The conclusion type is a
1820	  propositional equality of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\)-morphisms (whence
1821	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \Gamma(\alpha)\) and \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \alpha\) follow), not merely
1822	  an abstract-mate identification at the level of sheaves.
1823	\end{lemma}
1824	
1825	\begin{proof}
1826	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1827	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace,
1828	        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1829	        \leanok
1830	  The proof chains the four pieces into which the generator trace has been
1831	  decomposed. By the domain read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} the
1832	  source \(\Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde M)\bigr)\) is
1833	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) via \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\); by the codomain read
1834	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} — which rests on the
1835	  \texttt{pullbackSpecIso} identification of the cone legs
1836	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}, the single Mathlib-absent step —
1837	  the target \(\Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)\) is
1838	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) via \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). By the generator
1839	  trace Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace} the conjugated map
1840	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\) sends
1841	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). By
1842	  \texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul} (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}) this
1843	  assignment is exactly \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\); equivalently, in the
1844	  cancellation direction \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\) is
1845	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\).
1846	
1847	  Since \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is a Mathlib linear equivalence carrying
1848	  \emph{no} flatness hypothesis, the conjugated map is an isomorphism; being the
1849	  conjugate of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) by the two dictionary isomorphisms
1850	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}, \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\), it follows that
1851	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is itself an isomorphism. This closes the affine case
1852	  \emph{directly} on global sections, with no separate abstract-mate-versus-concrete-map
1853	  identification at the level of sheaves. (The full equality
1854	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
1855	  = \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\) of the intended signature follows from the
1856	  same chain once \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}, \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) are formed; the
1857	  formalized decl records the \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \Gamma(\alpha)\) corollary, which is
1858	  what the affine close consumes.)
1859	\end{proof}
1860	
1861	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1862	[Affine base change]
1863	  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
1864	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso}
1865	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_map_affine_local,
1866	        lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
1867	        lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange,
1868	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1869	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1870	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I)
1871	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L906--918).
1872	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
1873	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1874	  % Assume $f$ is affine.
1875	  % In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
1876	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
1877	  % (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
1878	  % we have
1879	  % $$
1880	  % g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
1881	  % $$"
1882	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1883	  change''.}
1884	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
1885	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then for every base-change square as
1886	  above the canonical map of
1887	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
1888	  \[
1889	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
1890	  \]
1891	  The quasi-coherence hypothesis on \(\mathcal{F}\) is essential and is not a mere
1892	  convenience: the proof works over an affine open by writing
1893	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for a module \(M\), and a general
1894	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form
1895	  \(\widetilde{M}\); without it neither the affine description of the pushforward
1896	  nor the tensor-associativity computation below is available, and the conclusion
1897	  fails.
1898	\end{lemma}
1899	
1900	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
1901	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
1902	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
1903	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
1904	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
1905	% for some $A$-module $M$.
1906	% We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
1907	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
1908	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
1909	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
1910	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
1911	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1912	% equality
1913	% $$
1914	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1915	% $$
1916	% as $R'$-modules."
1917	% NOTE: This affine lemma is closed directly on global sections, via the two named
1918	% obligations: lem:base_change_map_affine_local (locality reduction on the affine
1919	% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-
1920	% level value of the base-change map is cancelBaseChange). Both proved affine tilde
1921	% dictionaries -- lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso and lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso --
1922	% feed the second obligation. cancelBaseChange is in Mathlib with no flatness; the
1923	% affine case carries no flatness hypothesis. No Čech infrastructure is used here.
1924	\begin{proof}
1925	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
1926	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
1927	  global sections}.
1928	
1929	  \medskip
1930	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
1931	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
1932	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
1933	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
1934	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
1935	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
1936	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
1937	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
1938	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
1939	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
1940	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
1941	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
1942	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
1943	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
1944	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
1945	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
1946	
1947	  \medskip
1948	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
1949	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
1950	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
1951	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
1952	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
1953	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
1954	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
1955	  \[
1956	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
1957	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
1958	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
1959	  \]
1960	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
1961	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
1962	  of modules.
1963	
1964	  \medskip
1965	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
1966	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
1967	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
1968	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
1969	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
1970	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
1971	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
1972	  \[
1973	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1974	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
1975	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
1976	  \]
1977	  of \(R'\)-modules (Mathlib's
1978	  \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). This is the entire
1979	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
1980	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
1981	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
1982	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
1983	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
1984	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
1985	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
1986	  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).
1987	\end{proof}
1988	
1989	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
1990	
1991	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
1992	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
1993	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
1994	
1995	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
1996	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1997	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
1998	  \mathlibok
1999	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2000	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2001	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2002	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2003	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2004	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2005	  \[
2006	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2007	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2008	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2009	  \]
2010	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2011	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2012	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2013	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2014	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
2015	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
2016	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
2017	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
2018	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
2019	\end{lemma}

---analogies---
# Analogy: transparent `R'`-module action for a base-change-square `ModuleCat` iso

## Mode
cross-domain-inspiration

## Slug
fbc-base-change-square-transparent-module

## Iteration
009

## Structural problem (abstracted)
We need an isomorphism of `ModuleCat R'` objects realising a ring base-change /
restriction-extension square (`(A⊗_R R')⊗_A M ≅ R'⊗_R M`), built so that the `R'`-action on
the carriers is *usable by typeclass resolution* — i.e. `smul_zero`/`smul_add` fire — rather
than being an opaque `_aux` `Module` def produced by Mathlib's `ModuleCat.ExtendScalars` /
`RestrictScalars` functor objects. The question is the canonical Mathlib idiom for stating and
consuming such a square iso transparently.

## Empirical root cause (confirmed this iter, not merely suspected)
At the `refine LinearEquiv.toModuleIso (e := { g with map_smul' := ?_ })` site of
`base_change_mate_regroupEquiv` (`FlatBaseChange.lean:909`), the `R'`-module instances on the
carriers are the opaque
`base_change_mate_regroupEquiv._aux_3 / ._aux_5 : Module ↑R' ↑((ModuleCat.extendScalars …).obj M)`.
The two `r' • 0 = 0` zero branches of the `TensorProduct.induction_on` proof of `map_smul'`
**cannot close by any of** `simp only [smul_zero, map_zero]` ("simp made no progress"),
`rw [smul_zero]` ("did not find pattern `?a • 0`"), or `erw [smul_zero]`
("**failed to synthesize `SMulZeroClass ↑R' ↑((ModuleCat.extendScalars …).obj M)`**").
The headline is the last: `SMulZeroClass R'` does not synthesize *from* the opaque `_aux_3`
`Module R'` instance, so the `smul_zero` lemma cannot even be stated against the goal, let
alone rewritten. By contrast, the `add`/`tmul` branches close by `erw [smul_add]` /
`erw [ExtendScalars.smul_tmul]` because those lemmas unify against `r' • (a+b)` / `r' • (a⊗s)`
subterms already present in the goal (whose `SMul` instance is fixed by the term), needing no
fresh class synthesis. The asymmetry is intrinsic: `smul_zero`'s `0` carries no `SMul` subterm
to pin the instance, so the elaborator falls back to synthesising `SMulZeroClass` and dies on
the opaque `_aux`. `letI`/`inferInstanceAs`/`.toDistribMulAction` re-supply a *fresh* `_aux`
that TC still ignores or that `whnf`-times-out. **The in-place `map_smul'`-over-functor-object
route is therefore structurally dead, not merely hard.**

Note also: the optimistic note at `FlatBaseChange.lean:851` ("`exact LinearEquiv.toModuleIso
(base_change_regroup_linearEquiv ↑M)` typechecks once the helper is in a separate compiled
module") is **stale/wrong**; the detailed note at `:911` is **correct**. Tested directly: the
imported helper has type `(A⊗[R]R')⊗[A]M ≃ₗ[R'] R'⊗[R]M` with the *canonical* `Module A (A⊗R')`
(`Algebra.TensorProduct.leftAlgebra`) and `Module R M`, and `exact … .toModuleIso` fails with
"Application type mismatch … `(A⊗[R]R')⊗[A]M ≃ₗ[R'] …` but expected
`↑((ModuleCat.extendScalars includeLeftRingHom).obj M) ≃ₗ[R'] …`". The `⊗[A]` carriers differ
by the `Module A (A⊗R')` diamond (`includeLeftRingHom.toAlgebra` as used by `extendScalars`
vs `leftAlgebra` as used by `cancelBaseChange`); unifying them forces `whnf` of the opaque
object instance and does not go through.

## Failed approaches (from directive)
- Hand-built `g : LinearEquiv` over the bare functor-object carriers, `LinearEquiv.toModuleIso` →
  `map_smul'` zero branches blocked on opaque `Module ↑R'` (confirmed above).
- `erw [ExtendScalars.smul_tmul]` + `show` reductions → close `tmul`/`add`, leave the two `zero`
  branches with no tensor head to rewrite and no `SMulZeroClass` to synthesize.
- `letI`/`haveI`/`inferInstanceAs` re-supplying `SMulZeroClass`/`DistribMulAction` → fresh opaque
  `_aux`, ignored by TC; `.toDistribMulAction` projection `whnf`-times-out.

## Analogues found (ranked by porting cost, lowest first)

### Analogue: `Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.IsTensorProduct`)
- **Domain**: commutative algebra / ring base change (pushout of `CommRing`s).
- **Same structural problem there**: the *pushout-form* base-change cancellation. With
  `[Algebra.IsPushout R S A B]`,
  `cancelBaseChange R S A B M : B ⊗[A] M ≃ₗ[S] S ⊗[R] M`.
  Instantiated at `R=R, S=R', A=A, B=A⊗[R]R', M=M` it is **exactly the regroup equiv**
  `(A⊗[R]R') ⊗[A] M ≃ₗ[R'] R' ⊗[R] M`, and the `Algebra.IsPushout R R' A (A⊗[R]R')` instance
  it needs is supplied for free by `TensorProduct.isPushout'` (`Algebra.IsPushout R T S (R⊗S T)`
  with `S=A, T=R'`). Companions: `cancelBaseChange_tmul`, `cancelBaseChange_symm_tmul`,
  `toLinearEquiv_cancelBaseChangeAlg`.
- **Technique**: the equivalence is **natively `≃ₗ[S] = ≃ₗ[R']`** — Mathlib already discharges
  `R'`-linearity inside the bundled `LinearEquiv`. There is **no hand-written `map_smul'`**, hence
  **no `TensorProduct.induction_on`, hence no `r' • 0 = 0` zero branch at all**. This is the
  decisive structural improvement over the project's current core
  `TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange R A A M R'`, which is only `≃ₗ[A]` (its `B`
  slot is taken as `A`), forcing the project to *re-bundle* `R'`-linearity by hand via
  `rightAlgebra` + `comm` + a manual `map_smul'` — the exact source of the dead zero branches.
- **Mapping to project**: replace the `RegroupHelper.lean` core
  `base_change_regroup_linearEquiv` (`comm ≪≫ ATM.cancelBaseChange ≪≫ comm` + hand `map_smul'`)
  by `Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange ↑R ↑R' ↑A (↑A ⊗[↑R] ↑R') ↑M` (or keep the helper but
  swap its body). Then `base_change_mate_regroupEquiv` is `LinearEquiv.toModuleIso` of it, modulo
  the **one** residual obstruction shared by every approach: the `Module A (A⊗R')` diamond between
  `cancelBaseChange`'s canonical carrier and `extendScalars`'s functor-object carrier.
- **Porting cost**: low-to-medium. The R'-linearity problem evaporates; the only remaining work is
  the A-diamond carrier bridge (see "How to resolve the residual diamond" below), which is an
  *object-level* `≃ₗ`/`eqToIso` reconciliation that never writes an element-level `r' • 0`.
- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.

### Analogue: `ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App` / `restrictScalarsId` / `extendScalarsComp` / `extendScalarsId` / `restrictScalarsCongr` (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.ChangeOfRings`)
- **Domain**: category theory of change-of-rings functors.
- **Same structural problem there**: building a change-of-rings *tower* iso transparently. The key
  fact is that `restrictScalarsComp'App_hom_apply` / `_inv_apply` are **`= x` (the identity on
  the carrier set)** — the iso relabels the `Module` structure while fixing the underlying
  element. Hence an iso assembled from these never produces an element-level `r' • 0` goal: all
  module-action coherence is internal to the bundled `Iso`.
- **Technique**: assemble the comparison as a composite of `Iso`s in `ModuleCat R'`
  (`restrictScalarsComp'App` ×n, `extendScalarsId`/`Comp`, `restrictScalarsCongr` from a ring
  equation, `eqToIso`, `Functor.mapIso` of a smaller iso) — **never** `LinearEquiv.toModuleIso`
  of a hand `AddEquiv` + `map_smul'`. The project's own `gammaPushforwardIso`
  (`FlatBaseChange.lean:288`) is the existence proof that this route closes **axiom-clean** for a
  restrict-tower (`restrictScalarsComp'App` ×2 + `restrictScalarsCongr` + `eqToIso` from
  `globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop`).
- **Mapping to project**: use this family to (i) build the A-diamond bridge as an object-level
  iso, and (ii) if/when the square is restated functorially, glue the extend/restrict pieces.
  Caveat: a base-change *square* (Beck–Chevalley) mixes `extend` and `restrict`, so these tower
  isos alone do **not** produce the square — `cancelBaseChange` (analogue 1) supplies the genuine
  associativity content; this family supplies the transparent *packaging*.
- **Porting cost**: low (already in the project's toolbox; `gammaPushforwardIso` is the template).
- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.

### Analogue: `CommRingCat.moduleCatRestrictScalarsPseudofunctor` (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Pseudofunctor`) and `CategoryTheory.TwoSquare` / Guitart-exactness (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.GuitartExact.KanExtension`, `isIso_lanBaseChange_app_iff`)
- **Domain**: 2-category theory / pseudofunctors / mate-of-a-square (Beck–Chevalley) calculus.
- **Same structural problem there**: restriction of scalars as a *pseudofunctor*
